# Solar Array Maintenance Handbook

The Meridian facility operates four ground-mounted solar arrays that together supply sixty
percent of the campus baseline load, and this handbook defines the maintenance duties owned by
the site operations crew across all four of them. Array one occupies the southern slope behind
the logistics warehouse and carries the oldest inverters on site, which means its maintenance
window is scheduled first in every quarter and its spare parts shelf is stocked at double the
usual depth. Array two sits along the eastern fence line where dust accumulation from the gravel
access road is the dominant soiling factor, so its rinse cadence is monthly rather than
quarterly and its performance baseline is recalculated after every cleaning pass. Array three
was commissioned two years after the others and uses string inverters from a different vendor,
which is why its monitoring dashboard lives in a separate portal and why its alarm thresholds
do not match the values printed in the older laminated quick cards that still circulate in the
control room. Array four is the smallest of the group but feeds the emergency operations
building directly, and for that reason it is the only array whose maintenance requires a
switching plan approved by the utility interconnection engineer before any work begins. The
operations crew consists of two certified electricians, one controls technician, and a rotating
apprentice, and every task described in this handbook names which of those roles is permitted
to perform it, because the insurance policy that covers the campus distinguishes sharply
between electrical work and non-electrical work. Weather records for the site show that hail
larger than two centimeters has occurred four times in the last decade, and after each event
the entire glass surface of every array must be inspected within seventy-two hours, a
requirement that overrides every other scheduled activity except a declared electrical
emergency. The handbook is reviewed every January by the facilities director and the safety
officer together, and no page of it may be photocopied into toolbox binders, because stale
copies were the root cause of the mismatched torque values discovered during the audit two
summers ago. Contractors who visit the site for warranty work must be escorted at all times,
must sign the switching log even when they perform no switching, and must surrender their
temporary badges at the guardhouse rather than at the front desk, a rule introduced after a
badge was found in a vendor van three weeks after the associated work order had closed.
Performance data from all four arrays is exported to the energy office on the first business
day of each month, and the export script reconciles inverter counters against the revenue
meter before it sends anything, because the two sources drifted apart by almost four percent
during the winter the fiber link kept dropping. Spare module inventory is counted twice a
year, in March and in September, and any module removed from inventory for a swap must be
photographed front and back before installation so that the warranty file shows the exact
serial number and the condition of the backsheet on the day it entered service. The crew
maintains a standing agreement with the grounds team that vegetation between rows is cut to
ankle height before the summer inspection cycle begins, since shading losses from unmanaged
grass were measured at three percent of summer yield in the year before the agreement existed.
Every torque wrench used on the arrays is calibrated annually and carries a colored tag that
matches the calendar year, and a wrench with a missing or wrong-colored tag is treated as
uncalibrated no matter what its paperwork says, a convention borrowed from the aviation
maintenance world by the previous site lead. Lightning protection is tested in the spring by
an external firm, and the crew's only duty in that test is to open the marked disconnects in
the order listed on the laminated card inside panel L-2, then close them in the reverse order
after the firm confirms the ground resistance readings are within specification. Records of
every activity described above are kept in the maintenance management system for seven years,
and paper forms are scanned within five working days and then shredded, because the records
retention audit flagged loose paper as the single largest compliance risk in the facilities
department. New crew members shadow a certified electrician for their first thirty days
regardless of prior experience, and they may not sign a checklist as the responsible person
until the site lead has countersigned three of their supervised inspections without a single
correction. The handbook closes each section with the date of its last technical review, and
any section older than twenty-four months is automatically added to the January review agenda
even when nobody has reported a problem with it, because silent drift between documentation
and field practice is exactly the failure mode this document exists to prevent. Radio
communication during panel work uses channel four, the same channel monitored by the
guardhouse, and any loss of radio contact longer than ten minutes during energized work
requires the second crew member to walk the row and verify the worker visually, a rule that
has been invoked twice and justified itself both times. Finally, the crew keeps a plain-text
diary of anything unusual noticed on site, from new animal burrows near the cable trenches to
unfamiliar vehicles on the access road, because three of the most expensive repairs in the
site's history were preceded by small observations that at the time seemed too minor to file
as formal work orders.

## Quarterly Inspection Procedure

Every inspection starts with a thermal scan of the combiner boxes before any panel
is touched. Record ambient temperature and irradiance in the logbook, because both
values are required to normalize the performance readings.

- Verify torque on all busbar connections
- Photograph any discoloured MC4 connector
- Rinse panel surfaces with demineralized water only
- Log string-level voltage under open-circuit conditions
- File the signed checklist with facilities within two days

## Emergency Shutdown Criteria

# Vendor Warranty Contacts

Warranty claims for inverter faults go through the regional distributor, not the
manufacturer. Quote the site identifier MER-04 and the commissioning date in every
claim, and attach the last two quarterly checklists as evidence of compliant
maintenance.
